patches_submitted.subject: [RFC PATCH] mm: bypass swap readahead for zswap

version_history.subject: [RFC PATCH] mm: bypass swap readahead for zswap

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch bypasses swap readahead for zswap, an in-memory compression backend similar to zram. The current implementation of swap readahead is optimized for traditional disk-based swap devices and can cause issues like inflated anon residency, page cache thrashing, and increased fault latency when applied to an in-memory backend. By skipping readahead for zswap, the patch aims to improve performance by reducing unnecessary prefetching of pages that are not needed. The results from testing this bypass show significant improvements in build time, swap-in/out rates, and reduced swap readahead hits.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Nhat Pham expressed enthusiasm for exploring zswap optimizations and encouraged further investigation into potential improvements.

Reviewer Nhat Pham suggested modifying the readahead logic to skip zswap and sync-io devices at each slot in the readahead window, allowing IO work to be submitted while sparing synchronous decompression work that might affect latency.

Reviewer Nhat Pham questioned the performance benefits of bypassing swap readahead for zswap, pointing out that if the pages are needed, it transfers work from future page faults to the current one, and if they're not needed, it wastes cycles.
review_comments.raw_body:
You're playing with zswap? Nice :)

It does feel like we're leaving some obvious wins on the table with
zswap here, so any ideas are much appreciated :)

---

Yeah I can see the edge case you and Yosry brought up here. As we move
towards supporting multiple swap backends in the same system, that can
certainly happen. I'm hoping that locality will bail us out - pages
close together in virtual address space hopefully have similar
lifetime, access temperature, compressibility, and will go to the same
swap backend, etc. - but who knows :)

What if we still follow the readahead logic, but at each slot in the
readahead window, we check if the entry is owned by zswap or is of a
sync-io devices, in which case we skip the readahead...?

That way, we will still submit the IO work, but spare the ones that
require synchronous decompression work, which might affect latency...?

---

Hmm so what your proposal buys us is, if we're already doing readahead
(synchronously), might as well install the pages in the window into
the page tables? :)

Could you explain to me how does this improve performance? If the
pages are needed, we are transferring some work from future page
faults to the current page fault. If the pages are not needed, then we
are just wasting cycles right?

review_comments.author: Barry Song
review_comments.summary:
Barry Song noted that bypassing swap readahead for zswap may not be necessary as the original patch description already mentioned that zswap is an in-memory backend and should not trigger swap readahead, but he did not provide any specific technical concerns or suggestions.

Reviewer Barry Song expressed neutral sentiment towards the patch, stating that he is not opposed to either zram or zswap and acknowledging their importance in their respective domains.

Reviewer Barry Song expressed concern that adding a bypass for swap readahead in zswap would require the readahead path to check whether folios are in zswap or not, which he finds potentially complex.

Reviewer Barry Song noted that the approach could become complex and suggested either keeping the current patch or disabling readahead unconditionally when using zswap, without checking if the swap-in is coming from zswap.

Reviewer Barry Song noted that bypassing swap readahead for zswap avoids an additional page fault, which is faster than handling two separate faults, but increases the complexity of do_swap_page().

Reviewer Barry Song noted that bypassing swap readahead for zswap only results in performance improvements when using small page-cluster sizes (1), but causes regressions for larger page-clusters (>2). He observed a bigger benefit for sequential swap-ins with larger page-clusters.

Reviewer Barry Song did not provide any technical feedback or comments on the patch, instead sending a generic thank you message.
sentiment_signals: play with it from time to time
not trying to push for merge
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Nhat,

Sorry for the late reply. I've been quite busy recently.
I used to work on zswap when I was in the server industry. These days, I
still play with it from time to time, mostly for fun.

---

For my part, I'm not against either zram or zswap. They both play
important roles in their respective domains. :-)

---

That sounds fine to me. I'm just not sure how complex the code would
be. Would the readahead path need to check whether folios are in zswap
or not? That sounds like a pain.

---

Yes, but it seems like it could get quite complex. So I'm fine with
either the current approach in this patch, or simply disabling
readahead once we're sure we're using zswap, without even checking
whether the current swap-in is coming from zswap.

---

Yes, because it avoids another page fault. Handling two pages in a
single page fault is faster than handling two separate page faults,
each faulting in one page. Since we've already read the data out
synchronously, we don't want to fault on it again. The downside is
that it increases the complexity of do_swap_page().

I'm not even trying to sell my approach—I did it just for fun.
do_swap_page() is already quite complex, and my fault-around
implementation only makes it more complicated. So I don't really want
to push for it to be merged upstream. I think it will become much more
attractive once we have hardware-assisted decompression in
do_swap_page(), where we no longer spend CPU cycles on
decompression.

---

Yep. I'm only seeing improvements with page-cluster=1, which uses a
small readahead window. For page-cluster > 2, I'm seeing performance
regressions on workloads such as kernel builds. On the other hand, a
larger page-cluster provides a bigger benefit for sequential swap-ins.
So it's not a universal win.

---

Thanks for your kind words.

Best Regards
Barry

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] btrfs: fix a regression where PAGECACHE_TAG_DIRTY is never cleared
Re: [PATCH] btrfs: fix a regression where PAGECACHE_TAG_DIRTY is never cleared
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch addresses a regression in Btrfs where PAGECACHE_TAG_DIRTY is never cleared, leading to potential issues such as csum corruption when mmap writes are allowed on folios under writeback. The problem arises from the removal of folio_clear_dirty_for_io() and folio_mkclean(), which previously ensured that folios were write-protected during writeback. To fix this, the patch likely takes a lock in writeback to prevent races between truncate and writeback operations, ensuring that csums are correctly computed.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Qu Wenruo noted that truncation should wait for writeback to complete before invalidating a folio, and suggested exploring this idea without breaking existing functionality.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo expressed concern that the patch may introduce additional overhead and break cachestat(), requesting the v2 fix to be used as a hotfix first.
review_comments.raw_body:
Writeback still has the folio locked, so truncation should wait for the 
writeback first then invalidate it.

The other direction would also be true, if the invalidation invalidated 
the page first, then writeback should not got a dirty folio anymore.

But I can be totally wrong considering how complex things are.

I can explore if this idea won't break the existing runs first.

---

Sounds great. Feel free to include the v2 fix:
https://lore.kernel.org/linux-btrfs/d47af8cae38e32462d61cb62139c2732e4e8fdbe.1783407237.git.wqu@suse.com/T/

Although considering this is already breaking cachestat() and will cause 
extra overhead for writeback, I'd prefer the v2 as a hot fix first.

Thanks,
Qu

So we're still quite away from that.

No, no folio_clear_dirty_for_io() call anymore, all changes are inside

---

Mind to explain why folio_mkclean() is required? Is it for the same OE

Thanks,
Qu

review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that their previous explanation was rushed and agreed that the issue arises after i_size_write(), indicating they understand the problem but may need to revisit their patch

review_comments.raw_body:
Good point, but I think the issue is as soon as you do i_size_write(),
so the rest is just my explanation being a little misleading, I think.

---

yes, I think this path to the hang definitely requires the fact that
writeback does not hold i_rwsem while reading i_size.

---

Don't we risk weird truncate vs writeback races where they undo each
other, then? I assume taking the lock in writeback will be "correct" but
it might be slow or introduce some other locking order problem...

---

No it's a csum corruption with mmap being allowed to write to folios
that are under writeback (and thus computing csums)

Since we got rid of folio_clear_dirty_for_io() (and thus
folio_mkclean()), then if we mmap a large folio, then start writeback on
it, we never write protect the folios while they are under writeback so
the mmap user can trigger write faults and mess up the csums. We have
observed this in practice in our early testing of btrfs large folios in
production and we also have a racy reproducer that enables large folios,
mmaps a file and has a bunch of threads racing between touching each
sector with some new random data and fsyncing the file. We do that a bunch
of times, then drop caches and read everything. Do that whole thing in a
loop a few times and eventually you hit it. I can share the exact LLM
reproducer slop code if you would like, but that is the rough shape, to
explain the point.

I can send my holistic fix proposal with your v2 included in a series?

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Qu Wenruo pointed out that the patch's approach to clearing PAGECACHE_TAG_DIRTY is flawed, as it creates a timing issue between folio dirty and writeback flags changes. Specifically, if the folio dirty flag is cleared early, it can lead to losing the TOWRITE tag for the last block; if it's not cleared until the last dirty block clearing, folio_start_writeback() may have already been called, preventing the TOWRITE tag from being cleared.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo questioned the necessity of calling btrfs_mark_ordered_io_finished(), pointing out that the folio is already locked and the timing difference should not cause issues related to this function.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo noted that the patch introduces a deadlock caused by clearing dirty pages without calling btrfs_mark_ordered_io_finished(), which leads to leaked unfinished ordered extents and potential hangs on test cases btrfs/062 and btrfs/070. He suggested using folio_mkclean() instead of folio_clear_dirty_for_io() as a possible solution.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo identified two serious bugs in the original patch: a bug that causes clearing TOWRITE on non-sync writebacks and losing it for other pages in the folio, and a deadlock caused by truncating pages past the new file size without calling btrfs_mark_ordered_io_finished(). He suggested de-coupling PAGECACHE_TAG_DIRTY/TOWRITE from folio_*_writeback() calls to fix these issues.
review_comments.raw_body:
You're right on this changed btrfs_subpage_start_writeback() change.

But there is a deeper problem, the timing of folio dirty and writeback 
flags change is not synced anyway.

If we do not clear the folio dirty early, then the timing of folio dirty 
clearing is always on the last dirty block clearing.
But at that time, we may have already called folio_start_writeback(), 
thus for the last block we do not clear the TOWRITE tag.

If we do clear the folio dirty early, then it's exactly the problem 
you're describing.

So I believe we should not rely on btrfs_start/end_writeback() to handle 
the pagecache tags at all.

---

Mind to explain exactly where the missing 
btrfs_mark_ordered_io_finished() is?

The point here is, we always have the folio locked already, the 
difference is just in the timing where the folio dirty flag is cleared.

I didn't see a problem that the changed timing can cause problem related 
to btrfs_mark_ordered_io_finished().

---

I have not yet experienced btrfs/062 nor btrfs/070 hang.

[...]

---

I believe the root cause is that we can not rely on folio_*_writeback() 
calls to do the sub-folio level handling correctly.

I think the long term solution would be the iomap solution by tracking 
how many write bytes are still pending, and only call 
folio_end_writeback() once.

But for now, I think a quicker fix is, to de-couple the 
PAGECACHE_TAG_DIRTY/TOWRITE from folio_*_writeback().

The idea is, we clear PAGECACHE_TAG_DIRTY when the last subblock dirty 
flag is cleared.

Then do the same for PAGECACHE_TAG_TOWRITE, this time do not rely on 
__folio_end_writeback(), but manually do it inside 
btrfs_subpage_set_writeback().


I think this would be much smaller, and completely free us from the 
special handling of folio_*_writeback().

Thanks,
Qu

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author Boris Burkov addressed Qu Wenruo's feedback about a deadlock caused by clearing dirty flags too early, explaining that the issue arises from a race condition between truncate and writeback operations. He provided detailed explanations of the problem, including a sequence of events leading to the deadlock, and acknowledged that his previous fix was insufficient. However, he did not commit to revising the patch or providing a new solution.

The author acknowledges the reviewer's report of deadlocks in btrfs/062 and btrfs/070, but suggests that the issue may be due to a race condition rather than a bug in their patch. They confirm reproducing the issue on two systems and are still investigating the cause.

Author is addressing Qu Wenruo's concern about the patch causing a deadlock due to clearing DIRTY at an incorrect point in btrfs_subpage_set_writeback(). The author has been testing a hacky solution that clears both DIRTY and TOWRITE marks, but is still unsure if it's correct. A fix is planned.

Author clarifies that under his proposed solution, folio_clear_dirty_for_io() would still be called, and asks for confirmation on the importance of using folio_mkclean() to write protect the folio.
review_comments.raw_body:
Sorry, my explanation last night was rushed and clumsy, let me try to be
much more clear and specific!

The key change is your recent one, that went together with the removal
of folio_clear_dirty_for_io():

334509ce9d07 ("btrfs: use dirty flag to check if an ordered extent needs to be truncated")

and specifically the lines:

  /*
  * If the range is not dirty, the range has been submitted and
  * since we have waited for the writeback, endio has been
  * executed, thus we must skip the range to avoid double
  * accounting for the ordered extent.
  */
  if (!btrfs_folio_test_dirty(fs_info, folio, cur, range_len))
    goto next;


and quoting from the changelog:

  If the OE range is dirty, it means we have allocated an ordered extent but
  have not yet submitted the range. And that's exactly the case where we need
  to truncate the ordered extent.

But with this new fix, which returns folio_clear_dirty_for_io before
submission, the comment, skip, and changelog argument are no longer
true.

Suppose we dirty folios [F,F+K] at the end of a file and start writeback
on them, creating a single OE for [F, F+K]. extent_writepage will keep
looping through the folios submitting them.

Now suppose a truncate races in and shrinks i_size (taking i_rwsem but
no folio locks or blocking on OEs) via:
btrfs_setsize
  truncate_setsize
    i_size_write // shrinks i_size to M <= F
      truncate_pagecache
        folio_wait_writeback (not yet marked writeback)
        folio_invalidate
          btrfs_invalidate_folio (folio clean, skipped, this is the bug)

extent_writepage hits the code

  if (folio->index > end_index ||
    (folio->index == end_index && !pg_offset)) {
      folio_invalidate(folio, 0, folio_size(folio));
      folio_unlock(folio);
      return 0;
  }

which calls btrfs_folio_invalidate() which needs to mark the OE past
isize finished but skips it because the folio is clean (same issue as in
truncate above)

Now a bunch of processes, including a later call in the truncate itself,
will hang waiting on the OE which never finishes because these latter
folios never get submitted, nor does an invalidate truncate and finish
the lost OE.

Here are some trace printks showing the buggy sequence:
# btrfs_alloc_ordered_extent
91.901347  OE-CREATE      ino=306 oe_off=3637248 num_bytes=73728 isize=4587672
# btrfs_setsize the file, changing memory isize to newsize < oe_off
91.901392  TRUNCATE       ino=306 oldsize=4587672 newsize=2573711
# extent_writepage calls folio_invalidate for being past i_size (905 * 4096 = 3706880 > 2473711)
91.901528  WB-INVALIDATE  ino=306 folio_idx=905 isize=2573711 dirty=0 writeback=0
# btrfs_invalidate_folio does goto next and skips calling btrfs_mark_ordered_extent_truncated()
91.901531  SKIP-OE-FINISH ino=306 oe_off=3637248 bytes_left=73728 isize=2573711 writeback=0

I hope this is enough to figure out which one of us is missing
something! Sorry again for being so unclear earlier.

---

I have reproduced it on two systems with your patch (x86 4k pages for both)
and also while working on my own version which was basically your patch with
a hack to clean up the TOWRITE bit only in btrfs_subpage_set_writeback().

While working on the details of my explanation for this email, I was
adding lots of printks and it did go away in one build, so I guess the
race between truncate and writeback is finicky enough that you are
getting unlucky? I played with some delays but haven't yet found a
perfect place to make it 100% reliable.

---

I have been testing something hacky like this in
btrfs_subpage_set_writeback(), and I believe it is correct for TOWRITE
but still convincing myself about DIRTY. Just putting code out to be
more concrete. I think it's clearly hacky, but it's something we have
thrown around before so it was easy to start with:

 	keep_write = folio_test_dirty(folio);
 	if (!folio_test_writeback(folio))
 		__folio_start_writeback(folio, keep_write);
+	if (!keep_write) {
+		struct address_space *mapping = folio_mapping(folio);
+		unsigned long xas_flags;
+		XA_STATE(xas, &mapping->i_pages, folio->index);
+
+		xas_lock_irqsave(&xas, xas_flags);
+		xas_load(&xas);
+		xas_clear_mark(&xas, PAGECACHE_TAG_DIRTY);
+		xas_clear_mark(&xas, PAGECACHE_TAG_TOWRITE);
+		xas_unlock_irqrestore(&xas, xas_flags);
+	}
 	spin_unlock_irqrestore(&bfs->lock, flags);

---

Under your proposal would we still be calling folio_clear_dirty_for_io()
as in this current incomplete fix? You haven't commented yet either way
on your view of the importance of getting to a folio_mkclean() *somehow*
to write protect the folio, so I just wanted to make sure we were synced
on that, as that is what got me debugging this in the first place, not
the messed up PAGECACHE_TAG_DIRTY, which we obviously need to fix as
well!

Thanks,
Boris

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the problem lies in the interaction between truncate_pagecache and extent_writepage, where the former grabs the folio lock while the latter is holding it for writeback, causing a deadlock.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo questioned whether an inode lock is necessary to prevent stale results from i_size_read() during writeback operations, suggesting that if not locked, the writeback should proceed regardless of isize.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo expressed concerns about the patch's complexity and potential impact on beyond-isize handling, specifically mentioning the need to remove async-submission from compression.

The reviewer noted that the patch's fix for a regression where PAGECACHE_TAG_DIRTY is never cleared has an issue: when a truncate operation races with writeback, it can lead to a hang due to the folio being marked as clean and skipped by btrfs_folio_invalidate(), causing the ordered extent to never finish.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo asked for clarification on why folio_mkclean() is necessary, specifically inquiring if it's to prevent another instance of OE hang
sentiment_signals: questioning the necessity of an inode lock
review_comments.raw_body:
Just a small nitpick, the problem is not from the truncate_setsize() 
call site, as truncate_pagecache() is grabbing the folio with FGP_LOCK, 
so it can not grab the folio locked by writeback.

---

Thanks a lot! Now I understand how things are going wrong now.

But I still have a question, I thought we should have some kind of inode 
lock or something else to prevent i_size_read() from getting stale results.

And digging deeper, for the btrfs_setsize(), the inode is already 
locked. E.g. inside notify_change() there is a WARN_ON_ONCE() if the 
inode is not locked.

Would the root cause of the problem is that we did not lock the inode 
for writeback?

And if we do not have proper inode lock for writeback, then I think 
write back should never bother the isize check, and always do the 
writeback no matter isize.

---

That has a lot of extra requirements, and may have also change the 
timing of a lot of beyond-isize handling.

And the biggest requirement is to get rid of the cursed async-submission 
from compression.

So we're still quite away from that.

---

No, no folio_clear_dirty_for_io() call anymore, all changes are inside 
subpage.c now, just the extra PAGECACHE_TAG_* clearing during 
btrfs_subpage_set_writeback().

---

Mind to explain why folio_mkclean() is required? Is it for the same OE 
hang or something else?

Thanks,
Qu

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that their previous explanation was rushed and agreed that the issue arises after i_size_write(), indicating they understand the problem but may need to revisit their patch

Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2

Author expressed concern about potential truncate vs writeback races, suggesting that taking a lock in writeback may be necessary to prevent such issues.

Author addressed Qu Wenruo's concern about a regression where PAGECACHE_TAG_DIRTY is never cleared, explaining that the issue is actually csum corruption caused by mmap being allowed to write to folios under writeback, and plans to send a holistic fix proposal
sentiment_signals: acknowledged a mistake in their previous explanation
review_comments.raw_body:
Good point, but I think the issue is as soon as you do i_size_write(),
so the rest is just my explanation being a little misleading, I think.

---

yes, I think this path to the hang definitely requires the fact that
writeback does not hold i_rwsem while reading i_size.

---

Don't we risk weird truncate vs writeback races where they undo each
other, then? I assume taking the lock in writeback will be "correct" but
it might be slow or introduce some other locking order problem...

---

No it's a csum corruption with mmap being allowed to write to folios
that are under writeback (and thus computing csums)

Since we got rid of folio_clear_dirty_for_io() (and thus
folio_mkclean()), then if we mmap a large folio, then start writeback on
it, we never write protect the folios while they are under writeback so
the mmap user can trigger write faults and mess up the csums. We have
observed this in practice in our early testing of btrfs large folios in
production and we also have a racy reproducer that enables large folios,
mmaps a file and has a bunch of threads racing between touching each
sector with some new random data and fsyncing the file. We do that a bunch
of times, then drop caches and read everything. Do that whole thing in a
loop a few times and eventually you hit it. I can share the exact LLM
reproducer slop code if you would like, but that is the rough shape, to
explain the point.

I can send my holistic fix proposal with your v2 included in a series?

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Qu Wenruo noted that truncation should wait for writeback to complete before invalidating a folio, and suggested exploring this idea without breaking existing functionality.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo expressed concern that the patch may introduce additional overhead and break cachestat(), requesting the v2 fix to be used as a hotfix first.
review_comments.raw_body:
Writeback still has the folio locked, so truncation should wait for the 
writeback first then invalidate it.

The other direction would also be true, if the invalidation invalidated 
the page first, then writeback should not got a dirty folio anymore.

But I can be totally wrong considering how complex things are.

I can explore if this idea won't break the existing runs first.

---

Sounds great. Feel free to include the v2 fix:
https://lore.kernel.org/linux-btrfs/d47af8cae38e32462d61cb62139c2732e4e8fdbe.1783407237.git.wqu@suse.com/T/

Although considering this is already breaking cachestat() and will cause 
extra overhead for writeback, I'd prefer the v2 as a hot fix first.

Thanks,
Qu

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2, and plans to send a separate patch to fix csum corruption.
review_comments.raw_body:
I gave your v2 my r-b, and will send the patch to fix the corruption
next, separately. It shouldn't affect your patch, as it only conflicted
if we went back to folio_clear_dirty_for_io().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/2] syscall_user_dispatch: Make it configurable in Kconfig
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new Kconfig option, CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH, to make the Syscall User Dispatch feature configurable. The feature allows a thread's system calls to be intercepted and redirected to a userspace signal handler based on a prctl() configured instruction pointer range. This change makes it possible for users to disable this feature independently of the GENERIC_SYSCALL option. The patch modifies several files, including Kconfig, entry-common.h, syscall_user_dispatch.h, and others, to add the new configuration option and update related code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 2/2] syscall_user_dispatch: add kernel.syscall_user_dispatch sysctl
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a sysctl (kernel.syscall_user_dispatch) to control whether userspace can arm syscall user dispatch via prctl or ptrace. The default value is 1, allowing arming of syscall user dispatch. Disabling it while a task has already armed it does not cause the task's dispatch to become inactive, but rather remains active until the user attempts to disable/re-enable it via prctl or ptrace. This approach prevents undefined behavior in userland when translating non-Linux syscalls as Linux syscalls.

review_comments.author: tip-bot2 Price
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the sysctl for syscall_user_dispatch is not properly initialized, and requested to add a call to register_sysctl_init() in the late_initcall section.
review_comments.raw_body:
The following commit has been merged into the core/entry branch of tip:

Commit-ID:     34ef6308418f138395a664bf9f5d80ace9825ac6
Gitweb:        https://git.kernel.org/tip/34ef6308418f138395a664bf9f5d80ace9825ac6
Author:        Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net>
AuthorDate:    Mon, 06 Jul 2026 10:00:19 -04:00
Committer:     Thomas Gleixner <tglx@kernel.org>
CommitterDate: Tue, 07 Jul 2026 23:03:17 +02:00

syscall_user_dispatch: Add kernel.syscall_user_dispatch sysctl

Add a matching sysctl to go with CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH.

kernel.syscall_user_dispatch (default 1 - allow) controls whether
userspace may arm syscall user dispatch (both via prctl and ptrace).

Disarming is always permitted - same semantics as comparable knobs.

Disabling while a task has armed syscall user dispatch does not
cause it to become inactive - instead it remains active until the
user attempts to disable/re-enable via prctl or ptrace.  On the
next attempt to re-enable, the prctl/ptrace call fails gracefully.

The alternative would cause programs translating non-linux syscalls
to interpret those syscalls as linux syscalls, resulting in undefined
userland behavior.

Signed-off-by: Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net>
Signed-off-by: Thomas Gleixner <tglx@kernel.org>
Link: https://patch.msgid.link/20260706140020.873735-3-gourry@gourry.net
---
 Documentation/admin-guide/sysctl/kernel.rst | 17 +++++++++-
 kernel/entry/syscall_user_dispatch.c        | 38 ++++++++++++++++----
 2 files changed, 48 insertions(+), 7 deletions(-)

diff --git a/Documentation/admin-guide/sysctl/kernel.rst b/Documentation/admin-guide/sysctl/kernel.rst
index 9aed74e..2234644 100644
--- a/Documentation/admin-guide/sysctl/kernel.rst
+++ b/Documentation/admin-guide/sysctl/kernel.rst
@@ -1401,6 +1401,23 @@ Note that if you change this from 0 to 1, already created segments
 without users and with a dead originative process will be destroyed.
 
 
+syscall_user_dispatch
+=====================
+
+Controls whether userspace may arm Syscall User Dispatch via
+``prctl(PR_SET_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH, ...)`` or the
+``PTRACE_SET_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH_CONFIG`` ptrace request:
+
+  ==  ===================================================================
+  0   Arming syscall user dispatch is denied with ``-EPERM``.  Tasks that
+      already armed it keep it, and disabling it is always permitted.
+  1   (default) Arming syscall user dispatch is permitted.
+  ==  ===================================================================
+
+Only present when the kernel is built with ``CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH``
+and ``CONFIG_PROC_SYSCTL``.
+
+
 sysctl_writes_strict
 ====================
 
diff --git a/kernel/entry/syscall_user_dispatch.c b/kernel/entry/syscall_user_dispatch.c
index d89dffc..310c72e 100644
--- a/kernel/entry/syscall_user_dispatch.c
+++ b/kernel/entry/syscall_user_dispatch.c
@@ -3,19 +3,20 @@
  * Copyright (C) 2020 Collabora Ltd.
  */
 
+#include <asm/syscall.h>
+#include <linux/elf.h>
 #include <linux/entry-common.h>
-#include <linux/sched.h>
 #include <linux/prctl.h>
 #include <linux/ptrace.h>
-#include <linux/syscall_user_dispatch.h>
-#include <linux/uaccess.h>
-#include <linux/signal.h>
-#include <linux/elf.h>
-
+#include <linux/sched.h>
 #include <linux/sched/signal.h>
 #include <linux/sched/task_stack.h>
+#include <linux/signal.h>
+#include <linux/syscall_user_dispatch.h>
+#include <linux/sysctl.h>
+#include <linux/uaccess.h>
 
-#include <asm/syscall.h>
+static bool syscall_user_dispatch_allowed __read_mostly = true;
 
 static void trigger_sigsys(struct pt_regs *regs)
 {
@@ -102,6 +103,10 @@ static int task_set_syscall_user_dispatch(struct task_struct *task, unsigned lon
 		return -EINVAL;
 	}
 
+	/* Arming can be denied at runtime via sysctl, disarming is allowed */
+	if (mode != PR_SYS_DISPATCH_OFF && !syscall_user_dispatch_allowed)
+		return -EPERM;
+
 	/*
 	 * access_ok() will clear memory tags for tagged addresses
 	 * if current has memory tagging enabled.
@@ -172,3 +177,22 @@ int syscall_user_dispatch_set_config(struct task_struct *task, unsigned long siz
 	return task_set_syscall_user_dispatch(task, cfg.mode, cfg.offset, cfg.len,
 					      (char __user *)(uintptr_t)cfg.selector);
 }
+
+#ifdef CONFIG_PROC_SYSCTL
+static const struct ctl_table syscall_user_dispatch_sysctls[] = {
+	{
+		.procname	= "syscall_user_dispatch",
+		.data		= &syscall_user_dispatch_allowed,
+		.maxlen		= sizeof(syscall_user_dispatch_allowed),
+		.mode		= 0644,
+		.proc_handler	= proc_dobool,
+	},
+};
+
+static int __init syscall_user_dispatch_sysctl_init(void)
+{
+	register_sysctl_init("kernel", syscall_user_dispatch_sysctls);
+	return 0;
+}
+late_initcall(syscall_user_dispatch_sysctl_init);
+#endif /* CONFIG_PROC_SYSCTL */

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new configuration option, CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH, to make the Syscall User Dispatch feature configurable at both build and runtime. The feature is enabled by default at runtime, but can be disabled via prctl or ptrace calls. A sysctl interface, kernel.syscall_user_dispatch, is also added to control this feature dynamically.

review_comments.author: tip-bot2 Price
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the sysctl for syscall_user_dispatch_allowed is not initialized before it's used in task_set_syscall_user_dispatch(), which can lead to undefined behavior when a task attempts to arm or disarm syscall user dispatch at runtime.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/5] sunrpc: route to a populated pool in svc_pool_for_cpu()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the `svc_pool_for_cpu()` function in the sunrpc module to route incoming RPC requests to a populated pool, rather than one that is currently idle. This ensures that all incoming requests are serviced, even if some pools have no threads assigned to them. The change trades off NUMA locality for guaranteed service of incoming requests.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/5] sunrpc: hardcode pool_mode to pernode, remove other modes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch hardcodes the pool mode to pernode, removing other modes from the sunrpc code. The default behavior on multi-NUMA hosts changes to one pool per NUMA node, which is considered the right choice everywhere. This change simplifies the code by removing mode-based switch statements and ensures proper thread affinity and NUMA-local memory allocation. The patch also updates documentation and preserves backward compatibility for module parameters and netlink interfaces.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 3/5] sunrpc: guarantee a thread per pool when auto-distributing
series_items.patch_summary: This patch ensures that when distributing threads across multiple pools in the sunrpc service, no pool is left empty even if the total number of threads requested is less than the number of pools. This is achieved by guaranteeing at least one thread per pool, which may result in a total number of threads exceeding the requested count. The change only affects the auto-distribution path and does not impact explicit per-pool thread counts set via svc_set_pool_threads().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 4/5] sunrpc: tear down pool counters before dropping the pool map reference
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a potential memory leak in the sunrpc service by reordering the destruction of pool counters and the dropping of the pool map reference. The original order caused issues when the last pooled service was destroyed, as it would skip destroying the percpu counters. The patch ensures that the pool counters are destroyed while the pool map is still referenced, preventing a memory leak.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 5/5] sunrpc: derive the pool count instead of caching it in sv_nrpools
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the cached field sv_nrpools from svc_serv and instead derives the pool count using a new helper function svc_serv_nrpools(). The function uses the existing fields sv_is_pooled and svc_pool_map.npools to calculate the correct number of pools. This change is made possible by the fact that the pool mode is always per-node, making it safe to read npools without holding the mutex. The patch also removes a modulus operation from svc_pool_for_cpu(), which should improve performance.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series hardcodes the pool mode to per-node, removing other modes and addressing issues such as scheduling RPCs to pools with no threads. It also implements suggestions from Neil to eliminate the serv.sv_nrpools field, which has the side effect of reducing the modulus operations in the svc_pool_for_cpu() hotpath. The patches include fixes for comment cleanups, shortcoming of existing code, and auto-thread-placement case. The approach taken is to ensure that each populated node has at least one thread and prevent routing requests to empty pools.

review_comments.summary: Reviewer Laurent Pinchart suggested replacing the Assisted-by tag with a more concise format that includes only the LLM usage, such as 'Assisted-by: LLM # generate some test cases', and proposed rewriting the Attribution section of Documentation/process/coding-assistants.rst to emphasize the importance of documenting how generative AI was used. He also expressed support for removing the agent name and model from the tag.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Jul 03, 2026 at 02:12:40PM +0100, Lorenzo Stoakes wrote:

> > >>
> > >
> > > At the risk of being pedantic on a point where I think the document is
> > > kind of lacking:
> > >
> > > What level of assistance crosses the bar for an "Assisted-by: LLM" tag?
> > >
> > > Some sample levels of assistance to illustrate the point:
> > >
> > > 1. I used an llm to one-shot vibe-code a patch
> > > 2. I used an llm to write a patch but carefully reviewed every line
> > > 3. I used an llm to explore the design space for a patch but wrote it
> > > manually
> > > 4. I used an llm to debug or reproduce a kernel issue but then wrote the
> > > fix manually after fully understanding the defect
> > > 5. I used an llm to review a patch I wrote
> > > 6. I used an llm to research some chunk of code while writing a patch
> > > 7. I used Google while writing a patch and learned something valuable
> > > from the AI overview at the top
> > >
> > > I personally would 100% use the tag for 1 or 2, and have already done
> > > so. I have not been doing it for 3-5, as I think that will basically
> > > make every patch llm-assisted to the point of the distinction being
> > > meaningless. If we should be doing it for 3-5 (or some subset thereof)
> > > then my mistake and I will certainly start doing so. I would hope most
> > > people agree 6-7 and similar need no tag.
> 
> I personally think 1-2 are the only relevant cases.
> 
> > >
> > > Similar questions abound if you use an llm to help with writing the
> > > English text in the patch or emails.
> > >
> > > I have a feeling that this ambiguity is part of the reason we aren't all
> > > agreeing on the value of the tag?
> >
> > Yes, I raised something similar as reply to Christian's RFC [1], where I said
> > that for me the information *how* it was used is much more important:
> >
> > "
> > Assisted-by: LLM # translate commit message
> > Assisted-by: LLM # generate some test cases
> > Assisted-by: LLM # cleanup logic
> > Assisted-by: LLM # everything and I have no clue what any in here does
> > "
> 
> Yup, and we don't need complicated rules for that just 'document what you used
> it for and give a sense of how much'.
> 
> It's fuzzy but useful.
> 
> >
> > That tag is it stands is pretty useless, really.
> 
> Not to go over it all again but I disagree, even as it stands, it allows us to
> engage in conversation about the LLM usage if admitted, and to point those who

> 
> And it is a clear way to get the boolean 'is this person saying they used an

> But I agree with you it'd be MUCH more useful if we did the above.
> 
> I wonder if we could get consensus on adding a section to the doc saying that

I'd support a patch that replaces

Assisted-by: Claude:claude-3-opus coccinelle sparse

with

Assisted-by: LLM # generate some test cases

and rewrites the Attribution section of
Documentation/process/coding-assistants.rst accordingly.

I think most people in this mail thread have expressed that how
generative AI was used is the most important information, and several
people (including myself) have expressed a desire to stop the free
advertising. Unless I missed something, I don't think anyone has
expressed an interest in keeping the agent name and model.

> I can't imagine anybody would disagree with that, and that would get us positive

> > I assume most people only really use it for something in-between 1 and 2, but

Laurent Pinchart

review_comments.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
review_comments.summary: Lorenzo Stoakes expressed concerns that Jeff Layton's argument for removing LLM attribution requirements is flawed, as it only considers the lack of universal adherence to the rule and not its actual usage or value in facilitating engagement and data collection. He suggested a compromise where the requirement would be simplified to include only 'LLM' and a comment on the extent of AI-generated code, with an additional paragraph for subsystem-specific acceptance.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'm a little surprised I'm cc'd on this :) I'm not entirely sure if my pushing
back on this is going to mean anything but I suppose here goes nothing.

On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 10:32:48AM -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> We've had this requirement in place in the Documentation for several
> months, but it's becoming clear that the signal to noise ratio from this
> is quite low.
>
> 1/ It's not universally followed. While many people do try to attribute
> the LLMs in good faith, not everyone does for various reasons.

Does something not being universally followed therefore make it worthless?

You really have to explain that, because this is literally true of any rule
whatsoever we might have in the kernel, should we drop all of them?

I think you should replace this with a cogent argument such as that you feel it
is not being used in the _majority_ of cases or is very rarely used, and that
value is in your view not there.

>
> 2/ It basically serves as free advertising for proprietary LLM companies.

I agree with this point, we should drop the model.

>
> 3/ It's not clear why we want to collect this info in the first place.

Well I made arguments on the other thread, but to repeat:

- It makes it easier to engage with people when they do ack it.

- It makes it far quicker to be able to do so.

- There's a barrier to mentioning an LLM if it's not provided - people can get
  upset, or it can cause issues to raise it as a concern.

- Even if it's only there in _some_ cases, it makes _those_ cases easier to deal
  with.

- It provides some (incomplete) data that might make it easier to deal with
  bug-causing patches.

- It provides some (incomplete) data on bug rates with/without LLMs.

I do agree they're far far less useful when there's not some indication of how
much of the patch was LLM-generated.

Their usefulness is obviously deeply far from perfect, but not zero.

>
> Given that the data this provides is flawed at best and is being
> collected for a purpose that isn't clear, let's just kill the
> requirement for these tags from the kernel at large.

I feel there are purposes. Perhaps the argument is stronger for having the tags
on submissions rather than actually in-tree, however.

>
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

I think simplifying by requiring only 'LLM' and adding a line simply suggesting
a comment giving a sense of how much was used, and then perhaps a paragraph saying

	Subsystems may vary in their acceptance of this patch blah blah blah

Could be a compromise?

> ---
> Christian had proposed watering down the LLM attribution, but I think
> it's not productive to try and track this until we have a clearer sense
> of what we want to do with this information and how to make it more
> reliable.

Well he's moved now to stridently saying we should do what you're doing here so
presumably you're now in alignment.

review_comments.summary: Lorenzo Stoakes noted that the current tagging system for LLM attribution is flawed and has low value for real analysis due to self-reporting and a nebulous definition of what 'LLM was used' actually means, but still provides practical human value in signaling whether an LLM was used without being aggressive. He suggested using a standardized tag for this information instead of cluttering changelogs with tags.

> > of what we want to do with this information and how to make it more
> > reliable.
>
> I agree that the current tagging system is flawed and almost useless
> for real analysis -- self-reporting and a nebulous definition of what
> "LLM was used" actually means are red flags for any data scientist.
>
> I don't have a stand on whether the tagging should be removed or fixed.
> But today I ignore it (my 2-cents US) for these reasons.

Oh no doubt they have highly dubious value as _data_.

But they are useful for practical purposes :) i.e. 'ok I can talk about the
LLM-ness here without it being quite so aggressive to do so because it's
admitted'.

And also political value in that 'here is how you are supposed to do it'
vs. <how you did it>.

I think there's definite practical human stuff that they provide sufficient for
there to be value here, and I've found that in practice also :)

(Though of course it's far far from resolving how we deal with the unattributed
stuff, which remains an ongoing issue!)

>
> --
> Chuck Lever

Cheers, Lorenzo

At the risk of being pedantic on a point where I think the document is
kind of lacking:

What level of assistance crosses the bar for an "Assisted-by: LLM" tag?

Some sample levels of assistance to illustrate the point:

1. I used an llm to one-shot vibe-code a patch
2. I used an llm to write a patch but carefully reviewed every line
3. I used an llm to explore the design space for a patch but wrote it
manually
4. I used an llm to debug or reproduce a kernel issue but then wrote the
fix manually after fully understanding the defect
5. I used an llm to review a patch I wrote
6. I used an llm to research some chunk of code while writing a patch
7. I used Google while writing a patch and learned something valuable
from the AI overview at the top

I personally would 100% use the tag for 1 or 2, and have already done
so. I have not been doing it for 3-5, as I think that will basically
make every patch llm-assisted to the point of the distinction being
meaningless. If we should be doing it for 3-5 (or some subset thereof)
then my mistake and I will certainly start doing so. I would hope most
people agree 6-7 and similar need no tag.

Similar questions abound if you use an llm to help with writing the
English text in the patch or emails.

I have a feeling that this ambiguity is part of the reason we aren't all
agreeing on the value of the tag?

Thanks,
Boris

> Thanks, Lroenzo

review_comments.author: Theodore Tso
review_comments.summary: Theodore Tso noted that his workflow involves asking an LLM to modify code, but then manually reviewing and testing it before writing the commit description himself, which differs from the scenario where an LLM generates thousands of lines of code for a pull request. He emphasized that there's a significant distinction between modifying existing code with an LLM and 'vibe coding' a new OS from scratch.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 12:46:19PM -0500, Andreas Dilger wrote:
> It would make sense IMHO to address the ignorance in the most expedient manner,
> namely by telling the *LLM itself* to add this tag when it generates the commit
> message and/or reviews the code.
> 
> This could be achieved by adding a statement in "AGENTS.md" in the root of the
> source tree to this effect, or telling the agent to reference and follow rules
> in Documentation/process/coding-assistants.rst regarding the Assisted-by: tag.

This presupposes a particular workflow where the developer allows the
LLM to generate git commits.  I don't do that.  I'll ask the LLM to
modify the code, but then I'll generally fix it up --- very often by
hand because I can edit the sources than entering a prompt and waiting
for the LLM to figure out how to make the change.  I'll often run the
test cases myself, since if it takes 24 hours of VM time, and 2 hours
of wall clock time, I'm going to kick off the regression tests myself,
and verify the test results.

And I almost always write the commit description myself.  So when I
say, "Assisted-by" it literally is "Assisted by".  It is not "vibe
coded where the LLM generates thousands of lines of code that the
human being doesn't understand before sending ta pull request."  And
that's probably why you'll see people asserting that LLM generated
code can't possibly be accepted due to copyright reasons.  There's a
big difference between asking an LLM to modify already existing code
to add a feature, or to fix a bug, and to vibe code a new OS from
scratch.  One is far less likely to be a copyright violation than the
other.

						- Ted

review_comments.author: Mauro Chehab
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Mauro Chehab noted that the Assisted-by tag for LLMs does not provide useful information, as it is unclear whether the patch was generated by a specific model or tool, and suggested instead requiring patches to be written or reviewed by humans. He also proposed adding a warning in Sashiko's review process to identify AI-generated patches.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 3 Jul 2026 07:42:35 -0400
"Theodore Tso" <tytso@mit.edu> wrote:

> On Fri, Jul 03, 2026 at 09:30:28AM -0500, Greg KH wrote:
> > > So clarifying there that a "Assisted-by: LLM" is also good enough would make
> > > sense if we agree on that.  
> > 
> > If we all agree on that, I'm saying that _I_ don't care, but others
> > might.  
> 
> I agree that requiring the model name is not a good idea, because some
> developers might be using unreleased products where the name itself
> might not be public.  So allowing the version number of the LLM to be
> elided would be a good change.
> 
> I'll also note that it's starting to be the case where tool is
> actually quite relevant.  So it's not just about the LLM Model, but
> whether you're using Codex, Claude Code, OpenCode, or Shahiko.  You
> can use the tool or the harness with different models.
> 
> So whether the tag is:
> 
> Assisted-By: OpenCode:Gemini 3.5 Pro
> Assisted-By: Claude Code
> Assisted-By: LLM

I don't think an assisted-by for LLM actually helps.

I can't see any difference on adding it or doing something like:

	Assisted-By: smath
	Assisted-By: sparse
	Assisted-By: coverity
	Assisted-By: gcc
	...
	Assisted-By: my-favorite-web-search-engine

From my side, I don't care about whatever tool/process a developer has
to produce a patch. All I want is a proper patch that passes my review
criteria.

Also, identifying bad vibe-coding patches is usually easy: their 
descriptions look like written by a former literature professor that just 
took his first job as junior programmer: they come with useless comments,
random variable renames, unneeded code rewrites, etc.

Btw, if one wants them to be tagged as AI-produced, perhaps we can teach 
Sashiko to identify it and add a big warning on its review. This will
probably be more reliable than asking people to add a LLM tag.

What I think we should do, instead, is to let it clear that vibe coding
is forbidden - e.g. just asking AI to do something and sending it as-is
(*).
So, I would add something like this at
Documentation/process/coding-assistants.rst:

	"All patches shall be written or reviewed and modified by a human.
	 You should be able to understand exactly what the patch is doing,
	 why it is taking such approach and how. Pure non-trivial
	 machine-generated patches will be rejected."

(*) There are of course exceptions for it: typo fix patches for instance
    can be pure machine-generated. Even before AI, we have patches like
    that where people used some spell checker to fix typos. Again, they
    need human review before submission, as sometimes, spell checker fails
    when, for instance, it tries to replace an acronym with resembles
    a word.

-- 
Thanks,
Mauro

review_comments.author: Darrick Wong
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Darrick Wong suggested modifications to the Assisted-by tag format, proposing that for nondeterministic machine assistance, patch submitters note which parts of the patch had machine assistance applied, and introduced a new 'Mecha-assistance-done-by' or 'MAD' tag. He also emphasized the importance of good behavior in patch submission, such as cc'ing stable and responding to questions.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Jul 07, 2026 at 04:10:07PM +0100, Lorenzo Stoakes wrote:
> On Tue, Jul 07, 2026 at 03:29:44PM +0200, David Hildenbrand (Arm) wrote:
> > On 7/7/26 11:55, Lorenzo Stoakes wrote:
> > > LGTM! Do you want to send that then? People can comment on the actual path then
> > > (probably worth cc'ing everybody here on that also).
> >
> > Let's first gather some more thoughts on the rough direction before spinning of
> > yet another discussion. :)
> 
> I'm suggesting sending a simple, uncontroversial, change to get movement rather
> than continue this never-ending talking shop :)

Oh but I love shop talk! :P

My opinions (having been on vacation for most of this thread) are
roughly:

1. For patches generated by deterministic tools (e.g. sed/cocinelle),
please include the source code so that anyone reading the patch can
check the reproducibility of that patch.  But that can be free-form
in the commit message:

https://lore.kernel.org/linux-xfs/157343508488.1945685.9867882880040545380.stgit@magnolia/

2. For nondeterministic machine assistance, I like the idea of asking
patch submitters to note which part(s) of the patch had machine
assistance applied.  I don't care all that much about the technology
used (e.g. LLM, or Eliza, or whatever).  Something like:

Assisted-by: LLM # commit message
Assisted-by: LLM # finding bugs in the original commit

I don't care to give free advertising to any specific LLM-pusher, nor
do I care to give the whole *industry* any free publicity.  They can
spend their own leveraged money on advertising.

Mecha-assistance-done-by: finding bugs in the original commit

or maybe just

MAD: finding bugs in the original commit

:P

3. I grade *all* the trailers that submitters attach to patches!  And
how well they engage me on my weird followup questions!  Both help me to
construct a conscientiousness vibe, which is how I decide how much
effort to put into making a response.  Do you allege that your patch
fixes a bug but fail to cc stable?  Do you post obviously LLM generated
content but leave out an Assisted-by tag?  Do you habitually drop off
the list for long periods of time?  Decline to run /any/ QA on your
patches at all?  Not gonna waste my time.

OTOH, I also look for *good* behavior: cc'ing stable, responding to
questions with a day or two, kvetching on fstests@ about the horridness
of bash scripts?  I find that endearing. ;)

--D

> Anyway I'll leave it up to you!
> 
> Thanks, Lorenzo
>

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/3] fuse: don't clear folio uptodate on writethrough errors
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents the clearing of the 'uptodate' flag on folios in the fuse writethrough error path, which can lead to confidential information being leaked to userspace. The change ensures that if a write fails or is short, the page cache folio keeps the data written by the application instead of reverting to the server's contents on the next read. This fix addresses an issue where clearing the 'uptodate' flag could bring down the whole machine due to invariant violations in the mm code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 2/3] iomap: add helper to mark folio uptodate
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new helper function, `iomap_folio_mark_uptodate`, which marks a folio as uptodate and updates its uptodate bitmap if it has iomap state data attached. This is necessary for filesystems like fuse that need to mark a folio as uptodate outside of conventional iomap calls. The function simply calls the existing `iomap_set_range_uptodate` function with the entire folio range, making it an exported symbol. This patch addresses a specific use case where the iomap-internal uptodate bitmap needs to be synchronized with the folio's uptodate state.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 3/3] fuse: use iomap helper to mark folio uptodate
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) code to use a new helper function, iomap_folio_mark_uptodate(), when marking folios as uptodate. This is necessary because when large folios are enabled, the uptodate state of an iomap-backed folio can become out of sync if only the folio-level flag is updated. The patch addresses this issue by using the new helper function to update both the folio uptodate state and the iomap uptodate bitmap. This change ensures that the uptodate state remains consistent across both the folio and the iomap, even when large folios are enabled.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds a helper function to keep the uptodate bitmap in sync for filesystems that use iomap, specifically addressing the needs of fuse which may write data directly into the page cache outside of the normal read/write paths. The helper function, iomap_folio_mark_uptodate(), allows these filesystems to mark a folio as uptodate while also updating the internal iomap bitmap. This is necessary for fuse to enable large folios and has been implemented in three patches: one to update fuse call paths, another to add the helper function, and a third to remove an unnecessary helper for clearing uptodate state.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 1/2] mm/huge_memory: extend thp_underused() to mTHP folios
series_items.patch_summary: This patch extends the `thp_underused()` function to handle mTHP folios by scaling the threshold for zero-filled pages based on the folio size, using the existing `khugepaged_max_ptes_none` sysctl value. The logic remains unchanged for PMD-sized folios.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged a concern raised by Sashiko about potential integer division truncation issues and agreed to fix the issue in v2, keeping the original check on the raw `khugepaged_max_ptes_none` value.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Jul 7, 2026 at 1:18 PM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> thp_underused() decides whether a large folio on the deferred split
> list is underused and should be split so that its zero subpages can be
> reclaimed. However, the logic in it only accounts for PMD-sized folios.
>
> As preparatory work for splitting underused mTHP folios, make the logic
> in thp_underused() compatible with mTHP-sized folios. This uses the
> existing khugepaged_max_ptes_none sysctl value and scales it
> proportionally to the size of the folio as the threshold for how many
> zero-filled pages a folio may contain before it's considered underused.
>
> This introduces no functional changes for PMD-size folios.
>
> Suggested-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
> Signed-off-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>
> ---
>  mm/huge_memory.c | 12 ++++++++----
>  1 file changed, 8 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)
>
> diff --git a/mm/huge_memory.c b/mm/huge_memory.c
> index c0892cc533a9..ea5c350b4818 100644
> --- a/mm/huge_memory.c
> +++ b/mm/huge_memory.c
> @@ -4413,24 +4413,28 @@ static unsigned long deferred_split_count(struct shrinker *shrink,
>  static bool thp_underused(struct folio *folio)
>  {
>         int num_zero_pages = 0, num_filled_pages = 0;
> +       int nr_pages = folio_nr_pages(folio);
> +       unsigned int max_ptes_none;
>         int i;
>
> -       if (khugepaged_max_ptes_none == HPAGE_PMD_NR - 1)
> +       max_ptes_none = khugepaged_max_ptes_none * nr_pages / HPAGE_PMD_NR;
> +
> +       if (max_ptes_none >= nr_pages - 1)
>                 return false;

Andrew kindly directed me to a comment by Sashiko [1]:

"Does this scale calculation unintentionally disable underused splitting for
small mTHPs with valid sysctl settings?
Due to integer division truncation, max_ptes_none >= nr_pages - 1 can match
configurations that aren't the sysctl sentinel (HPAGE_PMD_NR - 1).
...
Should the sentinel check remain against the raw
khugepaged_max_ptes_none value before scaling?"

Sashiko is right. I'll fix this up in v2 and keep the original
khugepaged_max_ptes_none == HPAGE_PMD_NR - 1 check on the raw
non-scaled value.

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260707201735.4113107-1-joannelkoong@gmail.com

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 2/2] mm/memory: add anonymous mTHP folios to deferred split list
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds anonymous mTHP (huge page) folios to the deferred split list, ensuring that they are split and reclaimed when memory pressure is high. This addresses a problem where fully mapped zero-filled mTHPs were not being split by the deferred shrinker, leading to wasted memory. The patch minimizes overhead on the common fault path by using an inline check for large folios before adding them to the deferred list.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a memory regression issue on ARM by enabling the deferred split shrinker to reclaim underused anonymous mTHP folios. The first patch modifies thp_underused() to work for non-PMD-sized folios, scaling khugepaged_max_ptes_none proportionally to the folio size. The second patch adds anonymous mTHP folios to the deferred split list from the anonymous fault/collapse path.

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] fs/proc/task_mmu: fix PAGEMAP_SCAN written state for unpopulated ptes

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the PAGEMAP_SCAN functionality of the Linux kernel's /proc/task_mmu subsystem. Specifically, it ensures that unpopulated page table entries (pte_none) are consistently reported as written across different paths and scenarios. The patch also adds a selftest to verify this behavior. The issue was caused by a mismatch in how pte_none is handled between the fast path and the generic path, leading to incorrect reporting of write-protected pages. This patch resolves the problem by making the handling of pte_none consistent across all paths, ensuring that incremental-dump consumers can accurately track writes to memory ranges.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm/hugetlb: fix swap entry corruption when clearing uffd-wp at fork()

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/hugetlb: fix swap entry corruption when clearing uffd-wp at fork()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the Linux kernel's hugetlb module that causes swap entry corruption when clearing the uffd-wp bit during fork(). The issue arises because the present-PTE bit position is used to clear the uffd-wp bit, but swap entries store this state elsewhere. This leads to incorrect behavior when re-encoding the corrupted offset in certain paths, such as hugetlb_change_protection(). To fix this, the patch uses pte_swp_clear_uffd_wp() instead of huge_pte_clear_uffd_wp(), matching other functions that operate on non-present PTEs. This change ensures that the uffd-wp bit is correctly cleared in swap entries during fork().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/3] x86/tdx: Fix off-by-one in port I/O handling
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes an off-by-one error in port I/O handling for the x86/tdx architecture. The GENMASK macro was being used incorrectly, resulting in a mask that was one bit too wide for all I/O sizes. The fix involves subtracting 1 from the size when calculating the mask to ensure it is the correct width.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/3] x86/insn-eval: Add insn_assign_reg() helper
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new helper function called insn_assign_reg() to the x86 instruction evaluation module. The function writes a value into a register with x86 partial-register-write semantics, which means it handles different write sizes (1-8 bytes) and their corresponding effects on the destination register. This helper is needed by both KVM's instruction emulator and the TDX guest #VE handler for port I/O emulation. The patch lifts the existing assign_register() function from KVM into a standalone header file, making it reusable across different parts of the kernel.

review_comments.author: Sean Christopherson
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer noted that the comment about matching IN[BWL], MOV[BWL] etc. is confusingly placed and suggested rephrasing to make it clear that this behavior applies to all writes to GPRs as per the architectural definition, rather than being instruction-specific.

Reviewer Sean Christopherson suggested adding a comment to clarify the behavior of 4-byte writes on 64-bit CPUs, as he believes most people will initially think the code is buggy.
review_comments.raw_body:
The placement of the "(matching IN[BWL], MOV[BWL] etc.)" blurb is confusing.  I
*think* you're trying to say this behavior matches that of MOVB, MOVW, and MOVL
instruction mnemonics, but the blurb is buried in the snippet that specifically
describes the 4-byte write behavior.

FWIW, I think giving examples does more harm than good, because the behavior isn't
instruction specific, it's architectural behavior that applies to all writes to
GPRs, as defined in "3.4.1.1 General-Purpose Registers in 64-Bit Mode".  E.g. for
a MOV instruction that sign-extends a 32-bit immediate to a 64-bit registers, it's
not that the instruction is exempt from the normal GPR semenatics, it's that the
instruction performs a 64-bit write to the destination even though the source is
only 32 bits.

And the B/W/L terminology isn't architectural, it's AT&T syntax.  E.g. trying
to encode "movl" with NASM yields "error: instruction expected, found `movl dword'".
Yes, the kernel uses AT&T syntax for assembly, but I think this helper should very
explicitly document that it's emulating architectural behavior.

---

IMO, it's worth keeping a short comment here, because even with the explanation
above, I suspect most people will think the code is buggy.  E.g.

		/* As above, zero-extend 4-byte writes on 64-bit CPUs. */
		*reg = (u32)val;

review_comments.author: David Laight
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer David Laight noted that the new insn_assign_reg() function does not handle the case where the destination register is a segment register, which can lead to incorrect results when writing to it.

Reviewer suggested using bitwise AND (& 0xffffffff) instead of explicit casts to ensure correct behavior for 32-bit zero-extension, particularly in byte and short cases.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, 1 Jul 2026 07:59:05 -0700
Sean Christopherson <seanjc@google.com> wrote:

---

Or be even more specific and use '& 0xffffffff' rather than a cast.
Particularly since the casts of the RHS in the byte/short cases aren't
needed at all.

-- David

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author agreed to reword the comment in insn_assign_reg() to describe architectural GPR-write behavior and clarify that @bytes is the width of the write, not a property of the instruction.

Author Kiryl Shutsemau addressed David Laight's concern about the AH/CH/DH/BH clobber by deciding to keep the original KVM code and not rewriting it, as the previous attempt introduced the issue. He also updated the patch to reflect this change.

review_comments.author: Sean Christopherson
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Sean Christopherson argued that using a mask to handle the 4-byte case in insn_assign_reg() would defeat consistency, and instead suggested casting in the 1- and 2-byte cases for this reason.

Gave Acked-by
review_comments.raw_body:
+1.  My main argument for casting in the 1-byte and 2-byte cases is consistency
above all else, using a mask for the 4-byte case defeats that goal.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 3/3] x86/tdx: Fix zero-extension for 32-bit port I/O
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the x86/tdx code that handles 32-bit port I/O operations. The current implementation only masks the lower 32 bits of the result, preserving the upper 32 bits of RAX. However, according to x86 architecture rules, 32-bit operations should zero-extend the result to 64 bits. The patch uses insn_assign_reg() to write the result back into RAX with proper partial-register-write semantics, ensuring that the correct value is stored in the register. This fix addresses a problem introduced by a previous patch and has been reviewed by multiple developers.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series fixes bugs in the handling of emulated port I/O operations in the TDX guest #VE handler on x86 architecture. The first patch corrects an off-by-one error in the mask used for port I/O, while the second patch lifts a helper function from KVM to make it reusable by both KVM and the #VE handler. The third patch fixes 32-bit port IN operations by zero-extending them into RAX as per x86 semantics.

> > >
> > > Unfortunately I think the only way to "fix" the test is to allocate
> > > more memory, enough to trigger a flush on most interesting setups.
> > > Perhaps we should scale the amount of memory with the number of CPUs
> > > so that we don't have to keep playing whack-a-mole.
> >
> > I don't have a good idea for writeback, but for zswap out, would
> > MADV_PAGEOUT work here?
>
> I don't think the reclaim mechanism is the problem, but the fact that
> we don't have enough pending updates to flush the stats. Am I missing
> something?

Ah yeah, you're right. Hmmm.

Yeah it sucks, but maybe sleeping (more than the flush period) before
read is the only way. Which
is terribly implementation-dependent :(

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: Identified potential issues with the dependency on a swapfile being present and suggested starting a discussion to figure out the best way forward for everyone.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Jul 7, 2026 at 12:28 PM Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> On Tue, Jul 7, 2026 at 12:32 AM Baoquan He <baoquan.he@linux.dev> wrote:
> >
> > Currently zswap uses a per-swap-device xarray to map swap offsets to
> > zswap_entry pointers. This introduces an additional tree walk on every
> > zswap entry access.
> >
> > This series replaces the xarray with a "Pointer" entry type stored
> > directly in the swap cluster table. The new swp_tb_t variant embeds
> > a zswap_entry pointer plus a entry count, eliminating the xarray and
> > its lookup overhead entirely.
> >
>
> Have you had the chance to review the virtual swap work (latest
> version in [1])? At a first glance, it bears a lot of similarity to
> this.
>
> We're envisioning this to be the future architecture for zswap :) Is
> there anything that is missing that would satisfy your use case too?
>
> [1]: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260612193738.2183968-1-nphamcs@gmail.com/

Yeah this doubles down on the dependency of zswap on a swapfile being
present, but I guess the goal is for this to be complimented by your
other series to add ghost swapfile support. This was discussed at
length, and we don't really want to have a user-facing ghost/virtual
swapfile concept, at least not for now. Nhat's latest proposal is
essentially a ghost/virtual swapfile implementation that is
transparent to userspace, AFAICT.

As Nhat mentioned, if there are deficiencies in his proposal that are
addressed by yours, I think the most productive way forward is to
start a discussion (here or on Nhat's proposal) to figure out the best
way forward for everyone.

review_comments.summary: Responded to concerns about complexity and proposed a new design that merges the zswap tree into the swap table infrastructure, potentially simplifying the code paths.

> As Nhat mentioned, if there are deficiencies in his proposal that are
> addressed by yours, I think the most productive way forward is to
> start a discussion (here or on Nhat's proposal) to figure out the best

Now that I have taken another look at the other series too, I realized
that these 2 are really a big patch series, split into two. Vswap also
merges the zswap tree into the swap table infrastructure, and the code
paths are actually *simpler*: a lot of the swap-cache/swap refcounting
logic remains unchanged, whether the swap entry mapped to PTE belongs
to a virtual or physical swapfile.

With this new design:

1. Metadata can now live in struct zswap entry, if the swapfile is
zswap ghost swapfile.

2. You need to move the metadata around if you need to do zswap writeback.

It seems rather misleading to claim that the ghost zswap swapfile
design is simpler, when a lot of the complexity involved in this layer
merging is hidden in this optimization patch series.

If you're concerned about the xarray overhead, I'm willing to consider
the vmalloc array data structure. You can then graft the other
optimization in this series on to the vswap work.
